author: @matteonicoli

lo, lo, love this! 
What I can’t well figure out, and would love to start with this winter, is what pi or arduino you need, to working with heat sensors and motors, 12 v pumps to make a little 
outdoor rocket stove with indoor radiator.  
Just building a working principle isn’t sexy enough for me.  A little unit that works every day, would be so much fun.

author: @bikerfirefarter7280
text: I'd like to see how this would match up with internal scans, for printing things like dentures; or replacement skeleton parts, like bone splints etc, made of biodegradable materials. I've known numerous people who have had 'plates and rods' screwed to broken bones, and then months/years later had to have another surgery to remove said plates/rods when they became more hinderance than help.

author: @philipvecchio3292
text:
This reminds me a bit of the story about the first Cotton mills in America. At the time the factory owners thought that nobody could recreate their factories from memory, so this one Yankee went over and took a tour of a cotton mill in England and came back to America and built his own.

I say that because I have the feeling Rob is going to be coming home to heavily guarded industrial secrets from the 1800s.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol

author: @CCoburn3
text: They build huge ships in sections. And everything has to line up. Cabling troughs, plumbing pipes, bolt holes—EVERYTHING. And it costs a bundle to make those plans. This sort of scanner makes it possible to do the same sort of thing on a smaller scale at a MUCH lower price. All you have to do is build the first section and scan it. Then you can use the scan to help you design the other sections and they will line up perfectly. It sounds like a great tool.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: good thinking mate - cheers

author: @peterfelecan3639
text:
Interesting.
How about the time required for the scan itself and the time to edit the result.
What's the amount of editing required ?
You mentioned merging 3 STL files. Why is that needed ?
I understand that you promised to do more in depth videos about this product but what are the current results of above at first approach ?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you need to do more than one scan to get the bottom too - the software stitches it together to - the scan was a few minutes the editing a lot longer lol

replies.author: @peterfelecan3639
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Thank you.
The 3 scans had taken only a "few" minutes ? Really, how much longer was the edit ?
Was the edit done on the same device, i.e., the tablet ?
All that need to be taken in taken  consideration to evaluate the workload, don't you think ?

author: @mikaelhg
text: I was just thinking of how to create glass fibre renditions of car dashboards like this. You'd have to 3D scan the dashboard, and then turn the scan into a series of cross sections you could CNC out of plywood or aluminum. Then connect the cross sections with presized rods and presto. Cover the surfaces with paper and prime, and bam, a reusable, collapsible form for whole dashboards or dashboard sections.

author: @nathanaelsmith3553
text: Could be cool if scaling up small objects and printing them larger. One could for example print out that fox skull  in pieces at 5 times the size and join them together to make a giant one. Maybe in the future we will be scanning our own bodies and printing off our own clothes. Imagine scanning your feet and printing off some shoes.

replies.author: @OMIF1
replies.text: Or the opposite, you make something large so you can add detail easily, then shrink it down.

replies.author: @nathanaelsmith3553
replies.text: @OMIF1 that's what the royal mint  do with coins, They have a massive 50p coin blank made out of clay and a sculptor who carves the design in it. Not sure how they shrink it down though as that was before all this tech. Maybe they have a sort of milling pantograph?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: oddly enough shoes came to my mind too!

replies.text:
